The eldest ran to the window and peered through the wooden slats. There stood an old woman in traditional Korean dress—a long chima skirt and a jeogori jacket. She had a kind, wrinkled face and carried a basket of rice cakes on her arm. "She looks just like Grandmother," the eldest said, and he unlatched the door.

The old woman shuffled inside, smiling warmly. But as she crossed the threshold, the youngest brother's sharp eyes caught something. Beneath the hem of the long skirt, a striped tail—thick, bushy, unmistakably feline—trailed across the wooden floor.

"Brothers!" he screamed. "That is not Grandmother! Look at her tail!"

The figure straightened. Its face contorted—the kind wrinkles melted away, the jaw unhinged, and the face of a great tiger emerged, eyes burning yellow, fangs gleaming. The tiger had eaten the real grandmother and stolen her clothes.

The brothers fled in terror. The tiger sprang, claws scraping the floor, knocking over furniture. It caught the eldest first, then the second. But the youngest was small and quick. He ran to the kitchen, grabbed the heavy iron rice cake pot still warm from the morning's cooking, and hurled a steaming rice cake straight at the tiger's face.

The tiger roared, clawing at its eyes, blinded by the heat. The youngest brother darted out the back door and into the forest. He climbed a tall pine tree, his heart pounding, and watched from the branches as the tiger searched below, growling and sniffing.

Night fell. The moon rose. The tiger circled the tree for hours, but the youngest brother stayed silent and still. At last, the tiger gave up and slunk away into the dark mountains.

The youngest brother survived. He grew up cautious and wise, knowing that not every kind face hides a kind heart. Even today, they say, tigers roam Korean mountains—some still wearing grandmothers' clothes. But those who notice the tail beneath the skirt are the ones who live to tell the tale.

The story of the Tiger and the Three Brothers belongs to a cycle of Korean tiger tales that stretch back centuries. In Korean folklore, the tiger holds a complex place—both feared as a man-eater and respected as a guardian spirit, the mountain god's messenger. The tiger in this tale, like many in the tradition, embodies deception and hunger, the darkness that lurks at the edges of the village. Children hearing this story learn two things: to obey their mother's warnings, and to trust their own eyes even when everyone around them is fooled. The youngest brother lives not because he is strongest, but because he notices what others miss.
moral: Appearances deceive; cleverness saves.

title: The Monkey King's Wedding
summary: In ancient Sukhothai, at the edge of the great jungle, there lived a powerful monkey king named Hanuman—the greatest of all monkeys, able to leap mountains in a single bound.
full_text:
In ancient Sukhothai, at the edge of the great jungle, there lived a powerful monkey king named Hanuman—the greatest of all monkeys, able to leap mountains in a single bound. He had become lonely, watching the human villages, wishing for companionship.

One night, Hanuman used his powers to steal away the kings daughter, Princess Sita, bringing her to his palace in the trees. The king was devastated and offered his entire kingdom for her return.

But something strange happened. Princess Sita, terrified at first, came to know the monkey king. He was gentle, thoughtful, and surprisingly kind. He brought her the juiciest fruits, the sweetest water, and protected her from all dangers. Slowly, her fear turned to something like affection.

The monkey king fell deeply in love. 'You may return to your father, he told her one day. 'But promise me one thing—do not look back as you leave. My palace is beautiful only in my mind, and I am not handsome like your princes. If you look back, you will see only a monkey, and you will run in fear.

The princess promised and began her journey home. She walked through the jungle, the fruits of the trees heavy with sweetness, the flowers blooming in her honor. But curiosity crept into her heart.

Just before the village, she thought: 'Perhaps he is beautiful like a prince after all? He was so kind. She turned to look back at the palace in the trees.

She saw him then—truly saw him—a monkey, with fur and a tail, sitting sadly in the branches. She screamed and ran to her father's palace, never looking back again.

Hanuman wept that day, and it is said that even now, monkeys in Thailand sometimes cry at sunset, remembering their lost princess.

The lesson: Some beauties hide ugly truths—but kindness matters more than appearance.
moral: Appearance deceives.

title: The Dragon of Lopburi
summary: In the ancient city of Lopburi, there lived a powerful dragon beneath the great temple.
full_text:
In the ancient city of Lopburi, there lived a powerful dragon beneath the great temple. For generations, the dragon had protected the city from invaders, but in return, he demanded a terrible price: one young woman must be offered to him each year.

The people feared the dragon, but no one could defeat him. His scales were harder than diamonds, his breath could melt stone. Each year, families would mourn as their daughters were chosen by lottery.

But one year, a clever young woman named Niran volunteered. She was not beautiful in the traditional way—her father was a court jester—but she was the cleverest girl in the village.

She walked to the dragon's lair without fear. I have come to be your wife,' she said boldly.

The dragon, surprised by her courage, laughed—a great rumbling sound that shook the temple. You are amusing, little human. But you do not know what awaits you.'

Niran smiled. Perhaps, Great One, I can make you laugh instead of taking wives? Tell me—what makes dragons laugh?'

The dragon considered this. In all his centuries, no one had ever asked him a question. He told her jokes from dragon lands, stories of ancient battles.

Niran listened and laughed with him. Then she told her own jokes—jokes about humans, about the silly things people do. The dragon laughed so hard that his scales rattled.

You are the first human who has made me laugh in a thousand years!' the dragon declared. Go home, Niran. Return each year not as a sacrifice, but as my guest. Your laughter has freed all the daughters of Lopburi.'

And so it was. From that day forward, Niran visited the dragon each year with new jokes and stories, and the dragon protected the city even more fiercely. Laughter had conquered fear.

The lesson: Laughter conquers fear—and courage can defeat what strength cannot.
moral: Laughter conquers fear.

title: The Tortoise and the Hare
summary: In the sun-baked valleys where the dusty trails wound between olive groves and dry riverbeds, the animals of the district had gathered for their usual morning disputes.
full_text: In the sun-baked valleys where the dusty trails wound between olive groves and dry riverbeds, the animals of the district had gathered for their usual morning disputes. Chief among the arguers was a tall hare with flaring nostrils and a twitching whisker, who stood upon a flat stone and declared to all who would listen that there was not a creature in the land who could outrun him. "I have never been beaten," he boasted, "when I put forth my full speed. I challenge anyone here to race with me." The other animals shuffled their feet and said nothing, for they knew the hare spoke truly. But from the edge of the crowd, a low voice rumbled like a stone rolling downhill. "I accept your challenge." It was the tortoise, old and wrinkled, with a shell so weathered it looked like a boulder that had sat in the same place for a hundred years. The hare burst out laughing. "You? I could dance circles around you all the way to the finish and back again!" The tortoise merely blinked his slow, deliberate blink. "Keep your boasting until you have beaten me," he said. "Shall we race?" The news spread through the countryside like wildfire. The fox was appointed judge, and he marked the course with scented bushes, laying it across fields and through shallow ravines, a full mile from start to finish. At the signal, the hare shot forward like an arrow loosed from a bow, his long legs eating up the ground in great, effortless bounds. Within moments he had vanished over the first hill, leaving a cloud of dust behind him. The tortoise, meanwhile, began to move. He placed one heavy foot in front of the other, his shell swaying with each laborious step, and proceeded at a pace so slow that a watching beetle overtook him before he had gone ten yards. The hare, far ahead, looked back and saw no sign of his opponent. He chuckled to himself and thought, "There is no hurry. I could nap beneath this shady tree and still beat that old plodder with time to spare." So he lay down in the cool grass, folded his long legs beneath him, and within minutes was fast asleep, his whiskers twitching with pleasant dreams. The sun climbed higher and began its slow arc toward the west. The tortoise, who had never stopped moving for a single moment, passed the sleeping hare without a word. His eyes were fixed on the distant goal, and his heart beat with a steady, patient rhythm. On and on he went, his calloused feet scraping against the stones, sweat glistening on his leathery neck. The afternoon shadows lengthened, and at last the hare stirred. He yawned, stretched, and looked around. The finish line was still far away, but surely the tortoise was nowhere in sight. He turned and looked behind him. Nothing. He looked ahead again, and his blood ran cold. There, no more than a dozen paces from the winning post, was the tortoise, plodding forward with the same unhurried determination he had shown from the start. The hare sprang to his feet and ran as he had never run before. His legs pumped, his lungs burned, his heart hammered against his ribs. But it was too late. The tortoise crossed the finish line a full three strides ahead of him, turned around slowly, and said in his quiet, gravelly voice, "Plodding wins the race." And from that day forward, the hare never boasted of his speed again.
moral: Steady persistence triumphs over reckless speed.

title: The Legend of the Dreamcatcher
summary: Long ago, when the forests east of the Great Lakes stretched endlessly and the waters shone like mirrors beneath the moon, the Ojibwe people lived close to the land.
full_text:
Long ago, when the forests east of the Great Lakes stretched endlessly and the waters shone like mirrors beneath the moon, the Ojibwe people lived close to the land. They knew the language of the rivers and the ways of the animals. And watching over them was Asibikaashi, the Spider Woman, who wove the first webs in the corners of the birchbark lodges at dawn.

Asibikaashi was a guardian of the children. Each night, as the little ones fell asleep, she would spin a fine web above their sleeping places. The web was not to catch flies or dew, but to catch the troubled dreams that drifted through the darkness. The bad dreams, the ones of falling and being lost, would become tangled in her silken threads and dissolve with the first rays of the morning sun. The good dreams, the ones of flying and laughter, would slip through the small hole at the center of the web and float down to the sleeping child like a feather on still air.

As the seasons passed, the Ojibwe people spread across Turtle Island, filling the prophecy that had been given to them. They traveled to the four corners of the land, and Asibikaashi found it harder and harder to reach all the cradleboards where the infants slept. Her eight legs could only carry her so far, and the territory was vast.

So Asibikaashi came to the grandmothers of the villages in their dreams. She showed them how to bend a willow branch into a circle, the shape of the sun's journey across the sky. She showed them how to soak sinew from deer or cordage from nettle plants and weave it across the hoop in a spiral pattern, mimicking the web she wove each morning. "Make a small hole in the center," she said, "for the good dreams to pass through. Hang the web where the children sleep. I will be with you in every thread."

The grandmothers woke and gathered willow branches. They bent the wood into hoops and wove the sinew in the sacred pattern, spiraling from the outside inward, tying knots at each turn. Some tied eight points to honor Asibikaashi's eight legs, others tied seven for the Seven Prophecies. They hung feathers from the bottom: eagle feathers for courage, owl feathers for wisdom. They placed beads at the center, representing the spider herself.

They hung the dreamcatchers over the cradleboards, and the children slept peacefully. The bad dreams caught in the web were burned away by the morning light, and the good dreams passed through the hole, sliding down the feathers into the sleeping child's mind. The grandmothers taught their daughters, and the daughters taught their own children. The tradition spread through the villages, and Asibikaashi smiled from the Great Mystery, knowing that her protection would never end.

To this day, the Ojibwe say, if you wake before dawn and look closely at the dew-covered grass, you will see Asibikaashi's web sparkling in the first light. She is still weaving, still protecting the children. And the dreamcatcher, hung above the bed, still separates the darkness from the light, letting only the good dreams through.
moral: Good guides us; bad entangles.

title: The totem Pole
summary: Animals mark family history, telling the story of a clan through carved symbols.
full_text:
In the villages of the Pacific Northwest, along the misty shores where the great forests meet the sea, there lived many clans—the Bear clan, the Wolf clan, the Eagle clan, the Raven clan, and many more. Each clan traced its lineage back to the ancient ones, the first animals who had walked the earth in the time before time.

Each clan had its own stories—stories of great hunts, of battles won, of wisdom passed down through generations. These stories were too important to be forgotten, too sacred to be trusted to memory alone.

So the clansmen learned to carve. They gathered ancient cedars, the trees that had stood for hundreds of years, and with knives of bone and stone, they carved the faces of their ancestors. Each totem pole told a story—perhaps of a great-grandfather who had slain a whale, or a grandmother who had spoken with the spirits of the forest.

The Bear clan carved the great bear at the bottom, his paws protecting the family below. The Wolf clan carved wolves in pairs, watching over the village. The Eagle clan carved the great bird at the top, closest to the sky, for the eagles were the messengers of the gods.

When visitors came to the village, they would look at the totem poles and know exactly who lived there, what their family had accomplished, what stories they were proud of. The poles stood for generations, weathered by rain and wind but never forgotten.

The lesson: Remember your ancestors. Their stories are your foundation, and by carving them in wood, you ensure they are never lost.

Even today, the totem poles stand in the forests of the Pacific Northwest, telling their ancient stories to all who pass by.
moral: Ancestors should be remembered.

title: The First Corn
summary: The gods sacrifice themselves to feed humanity. Centers on Spirit,  human attempt to understand the soul, afterlife, and the unseen forces that govern existence..
full_text:
In the beginning, the world was dark. There were suns in the sky, but not the right kind—the first four suns had been destroyed because humanity was not yet worthy. The gods gathered to create the fifth sun and humanity itself.

The gods made humans from the stuff of the earth—mud, said some. But mud was too soft, too weak. The gods tried wood, but wood had no heart, no soul. Again they failed.

Finally, the gods tried the fifth element: maize. They ground the golden corn, mixed it with water and the sacred blood of sacrifice, and from this dough, they shaped the first humans. These were perfect—strong and wise, able to think and feel and love.

But there was still no light. The gods argued about who would become the sun, who would give up their life to light the world. One god, Nanahuatzin, stepped forward. 'I will jump into the fire, he said, 'and become the sun.

But another god, too proud, laughed at him. 'You are small and diseased. How can you become the great sun?

Nanahuatzin did not answer. He simply jumped into the great fire, and from his ashes rose the fifth sun—the sun we know today, giving light and warmth to all the world.

And the maize? The golden corn that the gods used to create humanity still feeds us today. Every grain is a gift from the gods, a reminder that life comes from sacrifice. When we eat tortilla or bread, we eat the body of the first humans, created from maize, blessed by the gods.

The lesson: Life comes from sacrifice. Nothing good is achieved without giving something of ourselves.
moral: Life comes from sacrifice.

title: The Lady Who Fell from the Sky
summary: Before the world was a world, before there were mountains or rivers or forests, there was only water.
full_text:
Before the world was a world, before there were mountains or rivers or forests, there was only water. Sky Country existed above the clouds, a beautiful land where the Sky People lived in peace. But in Sky Country, there was a sacred tree — the Tree of Light — that glowed with the power of creation. No one was permitted to touch it.

One day, a young Sky Woman was digging beneath the Tree of Light for bark to weave a basket. As she dug, she broke through the floor of Sky Country. The tree crashed down, its light scattering across the void. Sky Woman fell through the hole, tumbling through darkness toward the endless water below.

Two Water Birds — the Great Loon and the Snow Goose — saw her falling. They rose to catch her on their interwoven wings, and they called to the other water creatures for help. The Great Turtle rose from the deep and offered his back as a resting place.

"The woman needs land to stand on," said the Loon. "Who will dive to the bottom and bring up mud?"

The Otter tried first. He dove deep but returned empty-handed. The Beaver tried next, his broad tail slapping the water as he descended, but he too failed. The Muskrat, the smallest and humblest of the creatures, stepped forward. "Let me try," he squeaked.

The other animals laughed. "You are too small," they said. But the Muskrat dove anyway. He swam down through the darkness, past the bones of ancient sea creatures, past the roots of underwater mountains. His lungs burned. His paws grasped. And finally, at the very bottom, he scooped a single pawful of mud and kicked toward the surface.

He broke the surface gasping, the mud clutched to his chest. The other animals took the mud and spread it on Great Turtle's back. As Sky Woman stepped onto it, the mud grew and spread, forming the continents. This is why the Earth is called Turtle Island.

Sky Woman became the first mother of humanity. Her daughter gave birth to the Hero Twins who shaped the world further. To this day, the Iroquois and other Native peoples honor the Muskrat, whose humility created the land we walk on.
moral: Sacrifice feeds the world.

title: The Mysterious Traveler
summary: In the dusty roads of old Mexico, where the agave fields stretch silver under the moon and the hills echo with the songs of coyotes, there travels a figure who appears in times of need and disappears like morning mist.
full_text:
In the dusty roads of old Mexico, where the agave fields stretch silver under the moon and the hills echo with the songs of coyotes, there travels a figure who appears in times of need and disappears like morning mist. He is El Charro Negro, the Black Horseman, a mysterious stranger dressed in an elegant black charro suit with silver buttons that gleam faintly in the darkness. His horse, equally black and imposing, moves without sound, its hooves making no more noise than falling shadows. He appears at crossroads, on lonely roads, and at the edges of villages when the night is darkest, and he offers a simple proposition—wealth, power, and the solution to every problem, in exchange for a soul.

The legend says that El Charro Negro was once a man, a proud and wealthy landowner whose ambition knew no bounds. He made a pact with the devil for unlimited riches, but when death came to collect his debt, the man refused to pay. As punishment, his soul was condemned to wander the earth forever, riding from village to village, seeking to trap others in the same bargain that damned him. Others say he is the devil himself, riding the roads in human form to test the hearts of men and women.

The stories of his encounters are told around fires and in marketplaces, passed from grandparents to grandchildren as warnings. In one tale, a young farmer named Diego, struggling to feed his family, met the Charro Negro at a crossroads. The elegant horseman offered him a bag of gold coins that would solve all his problems. Diego's heart leaped with hope—but then he remembered the stories his elders told. Those who accepted the Charro's gold found it turned to leaves or ashes by morning. Their fortunes crumbled, their families suffered, and their souls were bound to eternal misfortune. Diego refused the offer, choosing honest poverty over cursed wealth. The Charro's eyes glowed red with anger for a moment, and then he vanished into the night, leaving only the smell of sulfur and a single scorched coin in the dust.

In another version, a greedy merchant named Don Felipe met the Charro on the road to Puebla. Blinded by the glint of gold, he accepted the stranger's offer and filled his pockets with shining coins. By morning, his fine garments had turned to rags, the gold had become ashes, and his body was found by the roadside, his eyes wide with terror. A boastful landowner who bragged that no devil could frighten him took the Charro's gold home to his hacienda. But at dawn, his house was found empty, with only hoofprints scorched into the courtyard, leading straight into the earth.

The Charro Negro also serves as a guardian of treasure. In some versions, he appears not to tempt but to test. He offers gold to the poor and watches their reaction. Those who refuse out of moral strength are blessed; those who accept with greed are cursed. He is a figure of judgment, appearing to those whose hearts are already corrupted by avarice.

Even today, travelers on Mexico's lonely roads speak of glimpsing a dark horseman at the edge of their vision, a silhouette of elegance and dread. The Charro Negro rides on, endlessly roaming, a reminder that wealth without wisdom invites ruin, and that the easiest path is rarely the right one. His legend endures because the temptation it warns against is timeless: the desire for more, the willingness to sacrifice everything for gain, and the terrible price that must eventually be paid.
moral: Be kind to strangers.

title: The Eternal Struggle: Quetzalcoatl
summary: Before the world as we know it existed, before the sun rose and set, before the first humans walked the earth, there were two brothers whose eternal struggle shaped the cosmos itself.
full_text:
Before the world as we know it existed, before the sun rose and set, before the first humans walked the earth, there were two brothers whose eternal struggle shaped the cosmos itself. They were Quetzalcoatl, the Feathered Serpent, bringer of wind, knowledge, and life, and Tezcatlipoca, the Smoking Mirror, lord of the night sky, sorcery, and change. They were opposites—light and dark, order and chaos, creation and destruction—and they could neither abide one another nor exist apart. Their rivalry is the engine that drives the universe through its cycles of birth, death, and rebirth.

In the beginning, the four Tezcatlipocas—the Black, the White, the Blue, and the Red—were born from Ometeotl, the supreme dual deity of Aztec mythology. Quetzalcoatl was the White Tezcatlipoca, and his brother was the Black. Together with their brothers Huitzilopochtli and Xipe Totec, they created the world from the body of the great earth monster Cipactli, a primeval creature part crocodile, part fish, who swam through the primordial waters. To lure her, Tezcatlipoca used his own foot as bait, and Cipactli ate it. The two brothers then captured her, tearing her body apart to form the heavens and the earth. Her skin became the grass and flowers, her eyes the springs and caves, her mouth the valleys and mountains. But the loss of his foot left Tezcatlipoca forever limping, and in some depictions he is shown with a smoking mirror in place of his missing limb.

The first age of the world, the First Sun, belonged to Tezcatlipoca. He transformed himself into the sun and ruled over a race of giants. But Quetzalcoatl, jealous of his brother's power, struck him from the sky with a stone club. Enraged, Tezcatlipoca turned into a jaguar and devoured the giants, ending the First Sun. Quetzalcoatl then became the sun of the Second Age, but his reign was also short-lived. Tezcatlipoca overthrew him, and this time Quetzalcoatl sent a great hurricane that turned the surviving humans into monkeys. The Third Sun belonged to Tlaloc, the rain god, but Tezcatlipoca stole his wife, and in his grief Tlaloc destroyed the world with fire. The Fourth Sun was ruled by Chalchiuhtlicue, the water goddess, but Tezcatlipoca accused her of pretending to be kind, and she wept so much that the world flooded, turning the survivors into fish.

The most famous confrontation between the two brothers took place in the legendary city of Tollan, where Quetzalcoatl ruled as the wise and beloved priest-king. Under his guidance, Tollan became a paradise. The maize grew so large that a man could not carry a single ear. Cotton came from the fields already dyed in brilliant colors. The people lived in peace and prosperity, and Quetzalcoatl asked them only for offerings of butterflies and snakes. But Tezcatlipoca, who had been watching from the shadows, descended from the heavens disguised as an old sorcerer. He came to Quetzalcoatl's palace bearing a gift—a mirror of black obsidian, polished until it shone. When Quetzalcoatl looked into the mirror, he saw for the first time his own face: an old man with sunken cheeks, wrinkled skin, and the unmistakable signs of mortality. The Feathered Serpent had thought himself ageless, but the mirror showed the truth. He cried out in horror.

Tezcatlipoca did not stop there. He brought pulque, the fermented drink of the maguey plant, and persuaded the abstinent king to drink. Drunk, Quetzalcoatl summoned his sister and lay with her in shame. When morning came and the king understood what he had done, his white robes were stained, and he could no longer rule. He burned his city, buried his treasures, and walked eastward toward the sea, weeping. At the shore, he either set himself on fire and rose into the sky as the morning star, or he built a raft of serpents and sailed away, promising to return in the year Ce Acatl—One Reed.

The Aztecs, who inherited this mythology, understood that the struggle between Quetzalcoatl and Tezcatlipoca was eternal and necessary. The world could not exist without both forces. Quetzalcoatl's wind softens Tezcatlipoca's harshness. Tezcatlipoca's jaguar keeps Quetzalcoatl's order from becoming stagnation. Creation requires destruction, and destruction makes way for creation. The Feathered Serpent and the Smoking Mirror are not enemies in the way we understand—they are the twin poles of existence itself. Their war is the pulse of the cosmos, and it will continue as long as the sun rises and sets.
moral: Balance is needed.

title: The Sword in the Stone
summary: After the death of Uther Pendragon, the kingdom of Britain fell into turmoil. Centers on Spirit,  human attempt to understand the soul, afterlife, and the unseen forces that govern existence..
full_text:
After the death of Uther Pendragon, the kingdom of Britain fell into turmoil. There was no crowned king, and every knight with enough men and enough ambition thought himself fit to sit on the throne. Law was broken on every side. The poor were trampled, the fields were burned, and there was no one strong enough to bring the evildoers to justice.

In those dark days, Merlin the prophet came to the Archbishop of Canterbury and took counsel with him. They agreed that all the lords and gentlemen of Britain should ride to London on Christmas Day, and there in the Great Church, they would pray for a sign from heaven. So it was done.

On Christmas morning, as the congregation emerged from mass, they saw a miracle in the churchyard. A great stone, four-square like a marble block, had appeared overnight. Set into it was an anvil of steel a foot high, and driven through the anvil into the stone was a fair sword, naked by the point. Letters of gold were inscribed around the sword, and they read: "Whoso pulleth out this sword of this stone and anvil is rightwise king born of all England."

The archbishop commanded the people to stay within the church and pray, and when mass was finished, all the lords and knights rushed to try their strength. The strongest knights in the realm took turns grasping the hilt, heaving with all their might. But the sword did not move so much as a hair's breadth. It held fast, waiting for the one who was meant to draw it.

The archbishop proclaimed a tournament for New Year's Day, so that the knights could prove their worth and perhaps the chosen king would reveal himself. The jousts were held in a great field outside London. Among the knights who came was Sir Ector, a nobleman of good standing, who brought with him his son Sir Kay, newly knighted, and his foster-son Arthur, who served as Kay's squire.

As they approached the tournament grounds, Sir Kay suddenly realized he had forgotten his sword at his father's lodgings. He called to Arthur. "Ride back and fetch my sword. I cannot joust without it." Arthur spurred his horse and galloped back to the inn, but the door was locked and everyone had gone to the tournament. Frustrated, Arthur thought of the sword in the churchyard. "My brother Kay shall not be without a sword this day," he said.

He rode to the church, tied his horse to the stile, and entered the churchyard. The stone stood alone, with the sword glittering in the winter light. Arthur did not know the inscription, did not know the prophecy, did not know that every knight in the realm had failed to move it. He simply grasped the handle and pulled. The sword slid from the stone as easily as a knife from butter. He took it, mounted his horse, and rode back to Sir Kay.

When Sir Kay saw the sword, he knew immediately what it was. He rode to his father and said, "Sir, I have the sword from the stone. I must be king of this land." But Sir Ector saw the truth. He made Kay swear on the Bible how he had come by the sword. When Kay confessed that Arthur had brought it, Sir Ector turned to his foster-son with tears in his eyes.

"How did you come by this sword?" he asked. Arthur told him the simple truth. Sir Ector then had Arthur replace the sword in the stone and watched him pull it out again, effortlessly. He and Kay knelt before the astonished boy. "Now I understand," said Sir Ector, "that you are the true king. God has chosen you." And so Arthur, who had only been a squire, was crowned King of Britain. The sword he drew that day would one day be known as Excalibur, and the kingdom he would build would be called Camelot.
moral: Destiny reveals the worthy.

title: The Holy Grail
summary: At the height of Camelot's glory, on the eve of Pentecost, the knights of the Round Table sat in the great hall at a celebration unlike any other.
full_text:
At the height of Camelot's glory, on the eve of Pentecost, the knights of the Round Table sat in the great hall at a celebration unlike any other. As they waited for a marvel to occur, a sudden clap of thunder shook the castle. A ray of sunlight brighter than any that had ever pierced the windows flooded the hall, and there, floating above the Round Table, appeared the Holy Grail, covered in white samite so radiant that no one could look directly at it. It filled the hall with the most exquisite aromas and placed before each knight the food and drink he most desired. Then it vanished, leaving the knights stunned and yearning.

Sir Gawain, the first to speak, rose and swore an oath: "I will go on the quest of the Holy Grail for a year and a day, and I will not return until I have seen it more clearly." One by one, every knight of the Round Table swore the same. King Arthur, seeing his fellowship about to scatter, wept. "You have undone me," he said. "The greatest fellowship the world has ever known will be broken by this quest."

The knights rode out across Britain, each following his own path. Many failed. Sir Gawain, brave but worldly, could not overcome his pride. Sir Lancelot, the greatest knight of all, was barred from the Grail's full revelation because of his adulterous love for Queen Guinevere. He entered the chamber where the Grail was kept, but a voice told him he could not enter further. He was struck down for three weeks and nearly died.

Only three knights succeeded. The first was Sir Galahad, the son of Lancelot, who had been raised in purity by nuns. From his first day at Camelot, he had sat in the Siege Perilous, the seat at the Round Table that would kill any unworthy knight who dared claim it. Galahad was the perfect knight, untouched by sin, his heart fixed entirely on heaven. He drew a sword from a stone that no other could move, and it was prophesied that he would achieve the Grail.

The second was Sir Perceval, the innocent one, who had been raised in the forest by his mother and knew nothing of knightly ways. His simplicity and faith carried him further than armor ever could. The third was Sir Bors, the only one of the three who would return to Camelot alive. He was flawed but penitent, and his humility earned him a place at the Grail's table.

Their journey led them through enchanted forests and perilous castles, to the court of the Maimed King, whose wound could only be healed by the Grail's presence. At last, in the mystical castle of Corbenic, Galahad was brought before the Holy Vessel. He knelt and received his Savior. Then he prayed that he might leave the world. His wish was granted, and angels bore his soul to heaven. Perceval became a hermit and died in contemplation. Only Bors returned to Camelot to tell the tale.

The Holy Grail was never seen again in Britain. It was taken up to heaven, leaving behind a broken fellowship but also a story of faith, purity, and the search for something holier than earthly glory. The quest for the Grail became the highest ideal of knighthood: to seek, to strive, and to find what lies beyond the reach of swords and crowns.
moral: Purity of heart is required for the sacred.

title: The Frog Prince
summary: In the days when wishes still worked and magic was as common as morning dew, there lived a king whose youngest daughter was so beautiful that the sun itself stopped to stare when she stepped into the garden.
full_text:
In the days when wishes still worked and magic was as common as morning dew, there lived a king whose youngest daughter was so beautiful that the sun itself stopped to stare when she stepped into the garden. She was kind-hearted but willful, used to getting whatever she desired.

Near the king's castle lay a dark, ancient forest, and in that forest was a well so deep that no one had ever seen the bottom. On hot summer days, the princess would sit by this well and play with her favorite toy: a golden ball that sparkled like captured sunlight.

One afternoon, she tossed the ball higher than usual, and it slipped through her fingers. It bounced once, twice, and rolled into the well. The princess peered into the darkness and heard a faint splash as the ball disappeared into the black water. She burst into tears.

A voice rose from the water. "Why do you weep, princess?" It was a frog. His skin was green and bumpy, his eyes bulged, and his voice was a croak. The princess was startled. "My golden ball has fallen into the well," she said.

"I will retrieve it for you," said the frog, "if you promise to let me be your companion. Let me eat from your plate and sleep on your pillow." The princess agreed without thinking, desperate for her ball. The frog dove, retrieved the golden ball, and tossed it onto the grass. The princess grabbed it and ran home without looking back.

That night, a knocking came at the castle door. The king opened it to find the frog on the step. "Your daughter made a promise," the frog said. The king listened to the story and told his daughter, "A promise is a promise." Reluctantly, she let the frog eat from her plate.

But when the frog demanded to sleep on her pillow, the princess lost her temper. She grabbed the frog and threw him against the wall. Instead of a dying croak, there was a flash of light. The frog transformed into a handsome prince. He had been cursed by a wicked witch, and only a princess who kept her promise could break the spell.

They were married the next day. The princess learned that true beauty is not about appearance but about honor. The frog taught her that every creature deserves dignity, and a promise, once given, must never be broken.
moral: Do not judge by appearances.

title: The Ugly Duckling
summary: It was summertime, and the countryside was beautiful.. A mother duck was sitting on her nest, waiting for her eggs to hatch.
full_text:
It was summertime, and the countryside was beautiful. A mother duck was sitting on her nest, waiting for her eggs to hatch. At last, the eggs began to crack—one by one, fluffy yellow ducklings tumbled out. But one egg remained, larger than all the others.

Finally, the great big egg cracked, and out came a large, gray duckling. He was homely and awkward—nothing like his pretty yellow siblings. The mother duck looked at him with surprise, wondering if he could really be hers.

The next day, the mother duck took all her children to the farmyard. There was a pond, and the ducklings swam beautifully. But the gray duckling was left behind, and the farmyard was full of mean ducks who pecked at him. 'He is so ugly! they quacked. Even his own brothers and sisters were unkind to him.

The ugly duckling was so sad that he ran away. He wandered through the fields and meadows, lonely and cold. He came to a pond where wild ducks lived. 'You are very ugly, said the wild ducks, 'but as long as you dont bother our families, you may stay.' A hunters dog came close, but when he saw the ugly duckling, he thought he was not worth catching and ran away.

The ugly duckling wandered on through bushes and tall grass. An old woman found him and put him in her oven to warm him, but she shooed him out in a hurry. He wandered through a garden where roses bloomed, and a swallow asked him why he was so ugly. But the swallow was shot by a hunter, and the ugly duckling was left alone again.

Winter came, and the pond froze. The ugly duckling swam round and round to keep a patch of water open, but eventually he fell exhausted into the ice. A kind farmer found him, picked him up with his bare hands, and brought him home to his wife and children. The children wanted to play with him roughly, but the kind farmer saved him.

At last, spring arrived. The ugly duckling saw three beautiful white birds flying toward him—swans! They were so graceful, so beautiful. The other birds on the pond quacked merrily, and the children called out, 'Look! There are new birds—see how beautiful! The ugly duckling bowed his head, hoping they would not notice his ugliness.

But what did he see reflected in the water? Not an ugly gray duckling—he saw a beautiful white swan! The other swans came to him and stroked his feathers with their beaks. Children ran to call everyone: 'Come look! Three new swans—there is a new one, the most beautiful! They threw bread and cake into the water.

The ugly duckling was so happy he had been through so much and had never given up hope. Now he was beautiful, and the swans welcomed him. He felt such joy—he had never known such happiness.

And so he lived happily, floating on the beautiful pond, surrounded by the love and admiration he had always dreamed of.
moral: What seems ugly may become beautiful.

title: The Little Mermaid
summary: A young mermaid princess sacrifices her voice for legs to win a prince's love. When he marries another, she faces a choice: kill him or become sea foam. She chooses sacrifice.
full_text:
Far out in the ocean, where the water is as blue as the petals of the loveliest cornflower and as clear as the purest glass, in the deepest depths where no anchor can reach, lay the kingdom of the Sea King. His palace rose from the ocean floor — walls of coral, windows of amber, a roof of mussel shells that opened and closed with the tide, each shell holding a pearl that would be the pride of any queen's crown.
Here lived the Sea King's six daughters, mermaid princesses, but the youngest was the most beautiful of all. Her skin was as soft as a rose petal, her eyes as blue as the deepest sea, and like all mermaids, she had no feet — her body ended in a gleaming fish tail. She was a quiet child, thoughtful and dreamy. She loved nothing more than to hear stories of the world above the waves, where flowers had fragrance, birds sang, and humans had immortal souls.
"Grandmother," she asked, "do humans live forever?"
"No, dear child," the old queen said. "They live far shorter than our three hundred years, but when they die, their souls rise to heaven. We, on the other hand, dissolve into sea foam and are no more."
The little mermaid gazed upward. "I would give all my three hundred years for one day as a human and a share in that eternal kingdom."
On her fifteenth birthday, she swam to the surface for the first time. A great ship lay at anchor, lanterns blazing. On deck, a young prince was celebrating. He was the most beautiful human she had ever seen — dark eyes, fair skin, a smile that made her heart tremble. She watched him all evening.
Then a storm came. Lightning split the sky, waves as tall as mountains broke over the ship. The prince was thrown into the churning sea. The little mermaid swam through the wreckage, her arms and tail burning with effort, and caught him just as he lost consciousness. She held his head above the water and swam through the raging storm until she reached a sandy beach near a white temple.
She laid him on the sand and kissed his forehead. When a young woman from the temple approached, the little mermaid slipped back into the water, hiding among the rocks. She watched as the woman revived the prince. When he opened his eyes, he smiled at her — believing she had saved him.
Heartbroken yet determined, the little mermaid swam to the cave of the Sea Witch. The witch lived in a house built of drowned sailors' bones, with a garden of poisonous flowers. "I know what you want," the witch cackled. "And I will help you — for a price. I will brew a potion that will give you legs. Every step you take will feel like walking on knives. And you will never return to the sea. If the prince marries another, you will dissolve into foam at dawn. But most precious of all — I must have your voice."
The little mermaid agreed. Her tongue was cut out, and she drank the potion. As the sun rose, she swam to the palace steps, where the change took her. Her tail split into two legs — and the pain was as the witch had promised, like a thousand knives.
The prince found her on the shore, naked and unable to speak. He took her into the palace, dressed her in silks, and grew fond of her. She danced for him, though every step felt like treading on sharp blades. She slept on a cushion at his door. But he did not love her — not as he loved the woman from the temple, whom he believed had saved his life.
The king commanded the prince to marry a princess from a neighboring kingdom. The prince went reluctantly, and when he saw the princess, he gasped — it was the woman from the temple. "Now I have found the one who saved me!" he cried. The wedding was celebrated.
On the wedding night, the little mermaid stood on the deck of the ship, holding the knife her sisters had traded their hair for. If she plunged it into the prince's heart, she would become a mermaid again. But she looked at his sleeping face, at his bride beside him, and she could not. She flung the knife into the sea, where it turned to blood-red foam.
At dawn, she threw herself into the water, expecting to dissolve. But instead, she rose into the air among the daughters of the air — ethereal beings who earn immortal souls through good deeds. "You have suffered and endured," they told her. "By doing good for three hundred years, you may obtain an immortal soul." And the little mermaid rose toward the sun, kissed the prince's bride unseen, and began her long journey toward eternal life.
moral: True love is about sacrifice, not possession - the little mermaid's selflessness earns her an immortal soul.

title: The Princess and the Pea
summary: Once upon a time, in a kingdom of rolling green hills and turreted castles, there lived a prince who searched the world for a true princess to marry.
full_text:
Once upon a time, in a kingdom of rolling green hills and turreted castles, there lived a prince who searched the world for a true princess to marry. He traveled to kingdoms near and far, meeting dozens of royal maidens, but something was always wrong with each one. Some were too proud, others too dull. Some lacked refinement, others were too calculating. After years of fruitless searching, the prince returned home heartbroken. "There are princesses enough in the world," he told his father the king, "but how can I tell which one is real?"
One evening, a terrible storm descended upon the kingdom. Thunder rolled like drums of the gods, lightning split the darkness, and rain fell in sheets so thick that the guards could barely see the castle walls. The wind howled through the turrets, and ancient oaks bent nearly to the ground. As the storm raged, a knocking echoed through the great hall. The old king himself answered, for he could not bear to leave any traveler stranded in such weather.
There stood a young woman, drenched and shivering. Water streamed from her hair and clothing, pooling at her bare feet. Her dress was plastered to her body, her face was pale with cold, and yet there was something in her bearing — a quiet dignity even in this bedraggled state. "I am a princess," she said through chattering teeth. "I lost my way in the storm. I beg shelter for the night."
The queen, wise and practical, was not easily convinced. She smiled warmly but said nothing of her doubts. Instead, she went to the guest chamber and devised a test. She stripped the bed of all its linens, placed a single dried pea on the bare mattress, then piled twenty mattresses on top of the pea, and then twenty eider-down feather beds on top of the mattresses. On this mountain of bedding, the princess was to sleep.
In the morning, the queen asked how she had rested. "Oh, very badly!" the princess exclaimed. "I scarcely closed my eyes all night. Heaven only knows what could be in that bed, but I was lying on something so hard that I am black and blue all over!"
Now they knew she was a true princess, for only a real princess could possess such delicate sensitivity as to feel a single pea through twenty mattresses and twenty feather beds. The prince married her at once, overjoyed that he had finally found his real princess. As for the pea, it was placed in the royal museum, where it may still be seen to this day — if no one has stolen it.
moral: Real is recognized.

title: Thumbelina
summary: There once lived a woman who wanted a tiny child more than anything in the world.
full_text:
There once lived a woman who wanted a tiny child more than anything in the world. She went to an old fairy woman, who gave her a barleycorn and told her to plant it in a flowerpot. The woman did as instructed, and immediately a beautiful flower sprouted — a tulip with petals of red and gold, wrapped tight as a blanket. The woman kissed the petals, and with a soft pop, the flower opened. At its center, on the green velvet stamens, sat a tiny girl, no larger than the woman's thumb. She was delicate as porcelain, with hair fine as spider silk and eyes bright as dew. The woman named her Thumbelina.
She made Thumbelina a bed from half a polished walnut shell, with a mattress of violet petals and a coverlet of rose petal. During the day, Thumbelina sailed across a plate of water on a tulip petal, using two white horsehairs as oars. Her singing was the sweetest ever heard — soft as a lullaby, clear as a bell.
One night, a great ugly toad hopped through the broken windowpane and saw Thumbelina sleeping. "What a pretty little wife for my son!" she croaked. She seized the walnut shell and carried it to the middle of a stream, where her son lived on a lily pad. The son was even uglier than his mother, covered in warts, but when he saw Thumbelina, all he could say was, "Croak, croak, croak!"
Thumbelina wept at the thought of marrying a toad and living in the mud. The fish in the stream took pity on her and nibbled the lily pad's stem until it broke free. Thumbelina drifted away downstream, leaving the toads far behind.
Her joy was short-lived. A large cockchafer beetle found her and carried her to a tree, where he introduced her to the other beetles. But they declared her hideous — she had only two legs, no feelers, and was shaped all wrong. The beetle, ashamed, abandoned her. Thumbelina wept alone. For the entire summer and autumn, she lived in the forest, eating nectar from flowers and drinking dew. She wove herself a hammock from grass blades and slept under a burdock leaf.
But winter came. The birds flew away, the flowers died, and the cold wind cut through her thin leaf clothing. Snow began to fall — each flake to her was as terrible as a shovelful of snow falling on a human. She wrapped herself in a withered leaf, trembling and hungry.
She found the door of a field mouse, who took her in. The mouse was kind but narrow-minded. "You may stay the winter," she said, "if you keep my house clean and tell me stories." Thumbelina agreed. One day, the mouse's neighbor, a wealthy mole, came to visit. He was blind and covered in dark velvet fur. He fell in love with Thumbelina at once, though he could not see her. He told her about his underground tunnels and showed her his vast stores of grain.
In one of the tunnels, Thumbelina found a swallow, frozen and seemingly dead. She nursed it back to health, warming it with stolen moments of care. As spring came, the swallow offered to take her away. "Come with me to warm countries where flowers bloom all year!"
But Thumbelina felt obligated to the field mouse. She stayed. The mole proposed marriage. Thumbelina was horrified at the thought of living underground forever, never seeing the sun. The field mouse forced her to prepare her dowry — spinning and weaving all summer.
On the wedding day, Thumbelina crept outside to say goodbye to the sun. "Farewell, bright sun!" she cried. At that moment, the swallow appeared. "Come with me now!" he urged. Thumbelina climbed onto his back, and they flew away over mountains and seas to a land of eternal warmth.
The swallow set her down in a bed of white marble among the most beautiful flowers in the world. In the center of the largest flower sat a tiny prince, no bigger than Thumbelina, with golden wings and a crown of diamonds on his head. He was the king of the flower angels. When he saw Thumbelina, he fell in love. He placed a crown on her head and gave her wings. They were married among the flowers, and her name was changed to Maia. And Thumbelina, who had been so small and so alone, finally found where she belonged.
moral: Small size does not limit a gentle heart.

title: The Red Shoes
summary: There was once a little girl named Karen, pretty and delicate, but so poor that she wore wooden shoes in winter that chafed her ankles raw.
full_text:
There was once a little girl named Karen, pretty and delicate, but so poor that she wore wooden shoes in winter that chafed her ankles raw. When her mother died, the only thing left was a pair of clumsy red shoes stitched from scraps of cloth. Karen wore those red shoes to her mother's funeral — not mourning shoes at all, but she had no others.
An old lady in a carriage saw the orphan and took pity on her. She brought Karen to her home, clothed her, fed her, and taught her to read and sew. The old lady was nearly blind and did not know the shoes had been red. When she saw them, she declared them hideous and had them burned. Karen was given proper clothing and grew up in comfort.
But she never forgot those red shoes. One day, she saw a pair in a shop window — red morocco leather, just like the shoes the princess had worn when the royal carriage passed through town. She begged the old lady to buy them for her confirmation, and the old lady, not seeing their color, agreed.
On confirmation day, Karen walked into the church in those red shoes. Everyone stared at her feet. She thought of nothing during the service — not the holy words, not the organ music — only the shoes. The old lady was told and was deeply shocked. "You must never wear them to church again," she said. "It is not proper."
But the next Sunday, Karen put on the red shoes anyway. An old soldier with a red beard stood outside the church and exclaimed, "What beautiful dancing shoes! They will stick fast when you dance!" Karen danced a few steps, and her feet began to move of their own accord. The shoes seemed to pull her into a wild dance she could not control. She danced through the church doors, down the path, and into the street. She could not stop.
The old lady fell ill, but Karen did not stay to nurse her. She put on the red shoes and went to a ball instead. The shoes danced her through the night, past midnight, into the dawn. She could not stop. She danced through fields and meadows, through brambles that tore her skin. The shoes danced her to the old lady's funeral, and past it, because she could not stop.
An angel appeared before her, white and terrible. "You shall dance," he said, "in your red shoes until you become pale and thin, until you are nothing but a skeleton. You shall dance from door to door, and wherever proud, vain children live, you shall knock and they shall fear you."
Karen begged for mercy, but the shoes danced her away. She danced through rain and sun, through day and night. Her feet bled. She grew thin and pale. At last, she crawled to the house of the executioner. "Cut off my feet!" she cried. "Cut off my feet and free me from this curse!"
The executioner did as she asked. The red shoes danced away with her severed feet, disappearing into the forest. But Karen had no feet now. She hobbled to the church on crutches and prayed. She prayed until her heart broke with remorse and her eyes ran with tears of repentance. "Have mercy on me, O God," she whispered.
The angel returned, but this time with a branch of roses. He touched Karen's head, and where he touched, peace flowed into her soul. She felt such light and love that her heart nearly burst. She was given a new pair of feet, not of flesh but of grace, and she lived out her days in quiet devotion, humble and grateful, never again looking at shoes with longing.
moral: Vanity leads to suffering; humility brings grace.

title: The Wild Swans
summary: Far away, in a land where swallows fly when winter comes, there lived a king who had eleven sons and one daughter named Elisa.
full_text:
Far away, in a land where swallows fly when winter comes, there lived a king who had eleven sons and one daughter named Elisa. The eleven princes went to school with stars on their chests and swords at their sides, writing with diamond pens on golden slates. Elisa sat on a little stool of polished glass, turning the pages of a picture book worth half a kingdom. They were the happiest children in the world.
But the king married a wicked queen who had no love for her stepchildren. On the very day of the wedding, she gave them sand in a teacup and told them to pretend it was cake. A week later, she sent Elisa to live with peasants in the countryside. Her hatred for the princes burned even hotter. "Go out into the world and fend for yourselves!" she shrieked, throwing enchanted dust over them. "Fly like great birds without voices!" The eleven princes were transformed into eleven magnificent wild swans. With a strange, mournful cry, they rose from the courtyard and flew into the deep forest.
Elisa grew up in the peasant cottage, dreaming of her brothers. At fifteen, she returned to the palace, but the queen, seeing her beauty, rubbed her face with walnut juice and tangled her hair with foul ointments until even her father did not recognize her. Heartbroken, Elisa slipped away into the forest. She wandered for days, eating berries, drinking from streams, sleeping on moss.
One evening, she came to a lake. At sunset, eleven white swans with golden crowns descended from the sky. As the sun sank behind the mountains, the swans shed their feathers and became her brothers. They wept and embraced, telling of their enchantment. "When the sun rises, we become swans," said the eldest. "Only at sunset do we regain human form. We live across the sea, in a land even more beautiful than this."
"We will carry you there tomorrow," they said.
At dawn, the brothers became swans again. They lifted Elisa on their backs and flew across the vast ocean. The journey was perilous — storms raged, and the sun burned. Elisa prayed for strength.
On the far shore, she had a dream. A fairy appeared, resembling the old woman who had given her berries in the forest. "Your brothers can be saved," the fairy said, "but you must have courage. You must weave eleven shirts from stinging nettles that grow in churchyards and on graves. While you weave, you must not speak a single word — not one — until the shirts are finished. If you speak, your brothers will die."
Elisa set to work. She gathered nettles that blistered her hands. She crushed them with her bare feet and wove the fibers into thread. Night after night she worked, her hands covered in blisters. Her brothers wept to see her suffering, but she could not speak to comfort them.
One day, a great king hunting in the forest found Elisa and fell in love with her. He took her to his palace and married her, though she never spoke. The archbishop grew suspicious of her silence. "She is a witch," he whispered. "She practices dark magic."
Elisa continued weaving in the palace, even as her hands grew raw. The nettles ran out, and she had to gather more from the churchyard at night. The archbishop followed her and saw her among the graves. "She is a witch!" he declared.
Elisa was condemned to be burned at the stake. On the morning of her execution, she was taken through the streets in a cart, still weaving the last shirt. The people jeered. The executioner seized her arm — but at that moment, eleven swans descended from the sky, circling overhead.
Elisa threw the nettle shirts over the swans. In an instant, they became her eleven brothers — all except the youngest, who still had a swan's wing instead of an arm, for she had not finished the last sleeve.
"Now I may speak!" Elisa cried. "I am innocent!" She told the king everything. The archbishop was banished, and the brothers were restored to their kingdom. Elisa and her brothers returned home, where they lived together in peace, their bond unbroken by the years of silence.
moral: Love endures any sacrifice.

title: The Fisherman and His Wife
summary: There was once a poor fisherman who lived with his wife, Ilsebill, in a miserable hovel by the sea — a place so small and filthy it was scarcely better than a pigsty.
full_text:
There was once a poor fisherman who lived with his wife, Ilsebill, in a miserable hovel by the sea — a place so small and filthy it was scarcely better than a pigsty. Every day the fisherman cast his line into the sparkling water, hoping for a decent meal.
One day, his line jerked downward with tremendous force. He pulled and pulled until he dragged up a magnificent flounder. But this was no ordinary fish. It looked at him with intelligent eyes and spoke: "I pray you, let me live. I am not a real flounder but an enchanted prince. What good would killing me do? Put me back, I beg you."
The startled but kindhearted fisherman said, "A fish that can talk I shall certainly let go." He released the flounder, who swam away leaving a trail of blood behind him.
When the fisherman returned home and told his wife, she was furious. "You caught a magical fish that grants wishes, and you asked for nothing? Look at this stinking hovel! Go back and demand a proper cottage!"
The fisherman did not want to, but his wife gave him no peace. He returned to the shore, where the sea had turned from clear to green and yellow. He called out, "Flounder, flounder in the sea, come, I pray thee, here to me. For my wife, good Ilsebill, wills not as I'd have her will."
The flounder appeared. "What does she want?" "A cottage," the fisherman whispered. "Go home," said the flounder. "She has it already."
When the fisherman returned, the hovel was replaced by a charming cottage with a flower garden, a yard full of ducks and chickens, and a well-stocked pantry. Ilsebill was delighted — for a week. Then she said, "This cottage is too small. Go back and demand a stone castle."
The fisherman went, though his heart was heavy. The sea was purple with rage. "Go home," said the flounder. "She has her castle."
Ilsebill now lived in a grand castle with a hundred servants, gold and silver furniture, and a park half a mile long. But she was not satisfied. "I want to be king," she declared. The fisherman protested, but she screamed until he went back. The sea was dark gray and churning. "She wants to be king," the fisherman said. "She is king already," the flounder replied.
Ilsebill sat on a throne of gold and diamonds. But the next morning, she woke the fisherman. "I want to be emperor — emperor of all the lands!" The fisherman begged her to be content. She threatened him. The sea was black and boiling. "She wants to be emperor," the fisherman told the flounder. "Go home. She is emperor."
Ilsebill sat on a throne of solid gold, guarded by soldiers from a hundred nations. Still, she was not satisfied. "Husband," she said, "go back to the flounder. Tell him I want to be pope." The fisherman's blood ran cold. "Wife, how can you be pope? There is only one pope in all of Christendom." "I will be that pope!" she screamed.
The sea raged in a tempest. The fisherman could barely stand. "She wants to be pope." "Go home. She is pope."
Ilsebill sat enthroned in St. Peter's, wearing the triple crown. Kings knelt before her. But that night, she could not sleep. "Husband! Go back! Tell the flounder I want to be like God — to command the sun and moon to rise at my will!"
The fisherman crawled to the shore through a hurricane. "She wants to be like God," he cried.
The flounder looked at him with ancient, weary eyes. "Go home. You will find her back in the pigsty."
And there she was, sitting in their original hovel, the stinking, filthy shack by the sea, where they lived for the rest of their days.
moral: Contentment is wisdom.

title: The Goose Girl
summary: There once lived an old queen whose husband had died years before.. She had one beautiful daughter, who was promised in marriage to a prince in a distant kingdom.
full_text:
There once lived an old queen whose husband had died years before. She had one beautiful daughter, who was promised in marriage to a prince in a distant kingdom. When the time came for the princess to depart, the queen prepared a rich dowry — jewels, gold, fine garments, and two horses: a common nag for the waiting-maid, and for the princess, a magnificent horse named Falada, who could speak. Before leaving, the queen pricked her finger, let three drops of blood fall onto a handkerchief, and tucked it into her daughter's bodice. "Keep this carefully," she said. "It will protect you on the road."
The princess tucked the handkerchief into her bosom and set out. As they rode through the countryside, the princess grew thirsty. "Pray, fetch me water from the stream in my golden cup," she said to her maid.
"If you are thirsty," the maid replied rudely, "dismount yourself and drink. I will be your maid no longer." The gentle princess obeyed without complaint. As she bent over the water, the handkerchief fell from her bodice and floated away. The maid saw this and rejoiced, for she now had power over her mistress.
The maid forced the princess to exchange clothes. She mounted Falada and threatened to kill the princess unless she swore never to tell anyone what had happened. The poor princess had no choice.
When they arrived at the prince's palace, the maid was welcomed as the bride, while the true princess was given the lowliest task: tending geese in the fields with a boy named Conrad.
The false bride feared Falada, who could speak and reveal the truth. She ordered the horse killed. The true princess begged the knacker to nail Falada's head above the dark gateway through which she passed each morning with the geese. As she passed the gate, she would say, "Falada, Falada, hanging there," and the horse's head would answer, "Alas, young queen, if your mother knew, her heart would break in two."
One day, Conrad noticed the goose girl talking to the horse's head. He also saw that her hair gleamed like spun gold when the wind lifted her bonnet. He told the king, who became curious. The next day, the king hid behind the gateway and heard the conversation between the girl and the horse's head.
The king called the goose girl before him. She threw herself at his feet, weeping, and told him everything — but she had sworn an oath of silence about the betrayal. So she told him only that she had been forced to take a terrible oath. The king, wise and kind, said, "If you cannot tell me, then tell this iron stove." He left her alone in a room with an old stove, and she poured out her heart to the iron grating, believing no one could hear. But the king had hidden a pipe that carried her voice to him in the next room. He heard everything.
The king ordered a great feast. He seated the false bride on one side and the true princess on the other. He told the story of a maid who had betrayed her mistress. "What punishment does such a woman deserve?" he asked. The false bride, not recognizing the story, answered, "She should be stripped naked and thrown into a barrel lined with sharp nails, dragged through the streets by wild horses."
"You have pronounced your own sentence," said the king. The false bride was dealt with as she had described. The true princess married the prince, and Falada's head was buried in the garden, where a tree grew in his memory — a tree that whispered the truth to anyone who would listen.
moral: Truth always revealed.

title: The Six Servants
summary: A prince travels with six extraordinary servants who each possess unique abilities (super hearing, super sight, super strength, etc.). Together they overcome impossible challenges to win a princess.
full_text:
Long ago, an aged queen who was a sorceress ruled over a kingdom with her daughter, the most beautiful maiden beneath the sun. But the old woman's heart was black. Whenever a suitor appeared, she set him three impossible tasks, and when he failed, his head was struck off. Many princes had died this way.
A certain king's son heard of the maiden's beauty and declared, "I will go and win her." His father forbade it, but the prince fell so ill with longing that for seven years he lay sick, and no physician could cure him. "Go, then," the father relented. The prince rose from his bed, instantly well, and joyfully set out.
As he rode across a heath, he saw what looked like a great heap of hay. Drawing closer, he realized it was the stomach of a man, enormous as a mountain. "If you need a servant," the fat giant rumbled, "take me." The prince asked his talents. "I can stretch myself to become even vaster, and I can eat and drink more than a thousand men."
Next, he met a man lying on the ground with his ear pressed to the earth. "I can hear the grass growing," the Listener said. "And I just heard someone being beheaded in the queen's castle far away."
Then came a man so tall his legs could stride over mountains. "I can see across the whole world," the Looker said. "At this moment I see a man in Cathay counting his coins."
They found a man with a cloth tied over his eyes. "Why do you cover them?" the prince asked. "Because my gaze shatters everything I see," said the Sharp-Eyed Man. "If I remove this cloth, I can reduce mountains to gravel."
Further on, a man sat shivering in the midday sun. "I feel cold," he said, "even though you burn." Yet when they passed a freezing glacier, steam rose from his body. The Hot-and-Cold Man could endure any temperature.
The sixth servant was a man whose neck stretched like a serpent's. He could see over the highest walls and reach across chasms.
The prince took all six into his service. They arrived at the sorceress's castle. "I wish to marry your daughter," the prince announced. "By all means," the queen smiled wickedly. "But first, fetch my ring from the bottom of the Red Sea."
The Listener heard where the ring lay, wedged between two rocks. The Tall Man strode to the shore. The Stout Man drank the entire sea in seven gulps, leaving the seabed dry. The Sharp-Eyed Man spotted the ring, and the Long-Necked Man reached down and plucked it up.
The queen was enraged but smiled. "Well done. Now, for the second task — eat three hundred oxen, hair, hide, bones, and all, and drink three hundred casks of wine to the last drop."
The Stout Man stepped forward. "Leave this to me." He devoured every ox, crunched every bone, drank every cask, and patted his stomach. "I could have managed more."
The queen's face darkened. "For the third task, you must guard my daughter through the night without sleep. If she is gone by morning, you die."
The prince sat with the princess, but she drugged his wine and slipped away. The Listener, however, heard her footsteps through twenty locked doors. The Long-Necked Man saw her hiding in a distant cave. The Tall Man brought her back before dawn. The prince had passed all three tasks.
The furious queen tried to burn the prince at the stake, but the Hot-and-Cold Man sat in the fire and shivered. When she sent her army to kill them, the Sharp-Eyed Man removed his blindfold and shattered the soldiers to rubble.
The prince married the princess, and the six servants, their work done, departed to seek their own fortunes. The prince never forgot that his greatest strength lay not in himself, but in the unique gifts of those he had chosen to stand beside him.
moral: Every person has unique talents - together, diverse abilities can overcome any obstacle.

title: The Donkey Skin
summary: A princess flees an unwanted marriage to her father by wearing a donkey skin disguise. She works as a servant until a prince falls in love with her true identity.
full_text:
In a magnificent kingdom, there lived a king who was the most powerful sovereign in all the land. His gardens overflowed with exotic flowers, his treasury with gold, and his stables held the finest horses. But the most marvelous creature in his possession was a donkey that produced gold coins instead of manure. Every morning, fresh gold glittered in the donkey's stall.
The king had a wife he loved dearly and a daughter who was the most beautiful princess in the world. But the queen fell gravely ill. On her deathbed, she made the king promise: "Only remarry if you find a woman more beautiful than I."
After the queen died, the king's advisors urged him to marry again. Suitors from across the land presented their daughters, but none matched the late queen's beauty. Then a terrible realization dawned: the only woman more beautiful was his own daughter.
The princess was horrified when her father declared his intention to marry her. She ran to her fairy godmother, the Lilac Fairy, who lived in a grotto deep in the forest. "Do not despair," the fairy said. "Tell your father you require a dress the color of the sky."
The king summoned the finest tailors, who produced a gown of celestial blue, embroidered with silver clouds and diamond stars. The princess demanded a dress the color of the moon — the king delivered one of silver light. She demanded a dress the color of the sun — he produced a gown woven from actual sunbeams, so radiant that those who looked upon it had to shield their eyes.
The princess had run out of excuses. "Ask for the skin of his gold-producing donkey," the fairy whispered. Surely this would stop him.
But the king, mad with love-obsession, killed the donkey and presented the skin to his daughter. The princess wept. "Now you must go," said the fairy. "Put on the donkey skin. It will make you ugly and unrecognizable. Travel to a distant kingdom and find work. No one will know you."
The princess covered herself in the shaggy, foul-smelling donkey skin. She traveled far, her face blackened with soot, until she reached a great city. She found work as a scullery maid on a farm, where she was called Donkey Skin. The other servants mocked her appearance and made her sleep in the attic.
One day, a prince from the neighboring kingdom came to the farm. He was bored and restless. He wandered into the scullery, and through a crack in the door, he saw Donkey Skin in her attic removing the filthy skin. Beneath it, she wore a dress of sunlight. Her beauty struck him like a physical blow.
The prince fell gravely ill with love. "I will only recover," he told his parents, "if Donkey Skin bakes me a cake with her own hands."
The princess, hearing this, baked him a cake. As she worked, a ring from her finger slipped into the batter — a gold ring with an emerald. The prince ate the cake, found the ring, and declared he would only marry the woman whose finger fit this ring.
Every woman in the kingdom tried. The ring fit no one — until Donkey Skin was brought before the prince. She slipped the ring onto her finger. Then she removed the donkey skin and stood before the court in her dress of moonlight.
The prince and princess were married. The princess's father, who had long since repented his madness, attended the wedding and was joyfully reunited with his daughter. The donkey skin was placed in a glass case in the palace, a reminder that true nobility can never truly be hidden.
moral: True nobility cannot be hidden - a princess in donkey skin is still a princess.

title: The Twelve Brothers
summary: A princess is born after twelve brothers who are cursed to become ravens if she speaks before her twelfth birthday. She must remain silent for twelve years to save them.
full_text:
A king and queen had twelve sons, all strong and healthy. The king, desperate for a daughter, made a terrible vow: if his thirteenth child was a girl, he would have all twelve sons killed so she alone would inherit the kingdom. He even ordered thirteen little coffins to be prepared, filled with wood shavings and laid in a locked room.
The queen discovered the plan. She called her youngest son, Benjamin. "Your father intends to kill you all if I have a daughter. Take your brothers and flee into the forest. I will raise a flag on the castle tower. White means a son has been born and you may return. Red means a daughter — and you must never come back."
The twelve brothers fled to the forest and hid in a cave. After twelve days, a red flag was raised. The brothers were devastated. "Our sister will be our death!" they cried in anger. Benjamin tried to calm them, but they swore revenge on every girl they met. Deep in the forest, they found an enchanted cottage and lived there together, hunting and foraging, their hearts hardened by bitterness.
Meanwhile, the princess grew up not knowing about her brothers. She was beautiful and kind, with a golden star on her forehead. One day, she discovered the thirteen coffins in the locked room and asked her mother about them. The queen, weeping, told her everything.
"I will find them and bring them home," the princess declared. She gathered her belongings and walked into the forest, searching for days. At last, she found the enchanted cottage. Benjamin answered the door. "I am your sister," she said. When the brothers returned, they were furious at first, but seeing her star and her gentleness, their anger melted. They loved her and welcomed her.
The princess stayed with them, keeping house while they hunted. In the garden grew twelve white lilies. She picked them to give her brothers a gift — and at once, the brothers were turned into twelve ravens, their cottage vanished, and the princess was alone.
An old woman appeared. "Foolish child," she said. "Those lilies were your brothers' souls. Now you must make amends. To save them, you must remain completely silent for twelve years — not a word, not a sound. And you must sew twelve shirts from starflowers and nettles."
The princess set to work. She gathered nettles that stung her hands raw and starflowers that bloomed only under moonlight. She sat beneath a tree and wove, speaking not a word.
A king hunting in the forest found her. She was so beautiful that he fell in love and brought her to his castle. Despite her silence, he married her. His mother, the queen dowager, hated the silent bride. "She is a witch," she whispered. "Her silence proves her evil."
The princess gave birth to a son. The queen dowager stole the baby and claimed the princess had killed it. Still, the princess said nothing. She continued weaving her shirts.
A second son was born, and again the queen dowager stole him. Still the princess remained silent. She was put on trial for murder and witchcraft. The older queen produced false witnesses. The king, heartbroken, was forced to sentence her to be burned at the stake.
On the day of her execution, the princess was led to the pyre. She had sewn all twelve shirts except for one, which still lacked a left sleeve. As she walked, twelve ravens descended from the sky. She threw the shirts over them — and they became her twelve brothers, whole and human. The youngest still had a raven's wing instead of a left arm.
"Now I may speak!" she cried, and she told the king everything. The queen dowager was thrown into a pit of vipers for her crimes. The king embraced his wife, and the twelve brothers were restored to their rightful place. The princess had broken the curse — not through words, but through the most powerful language of all: silent, devoted love.
moral: Silent sacrifice and sisterly devotion can break the most powerful curses.

title: The Wolf and the Seven Kids
summary: There was once an old mother goat who lived with her seven little kids in a cozy cottage at the edge of a great forest.
full_text:
There was once an old mother goat who lived with her seven little kids in a cozy cottage at the edge of a great forest. She loved her children dearly and warned them constantly. One morning she needed to gather food, so she called the seven kids around her.
